## Assistant

Mostly right, with one correction: there are tool crates already, but there is not yet a general agent tool-calling runtime wired through chat.

What exists:

- [crates/tool-registry](/home/cachebag/rafael/crates/tool-registry/src/lib.rs:115): generic registry/scaffold for tool definitions, OpenAI tool specs, and invocation validation.
- [crates/tools/filesystem](/home/cachebag/rafael/crates/tools/filesystem/src/lib.rs:29): actual filesystem operations.
- [crates/tools/git](/home/cachebag/rafael/crates/tools/git/src/lib.rs:17): git wrapper.
- [crates/tools/shell](/home/cachebag/rafael/crates/tools/shell/src/lib.rs:45): safe-ish command runner.
- [crates/tools/systemd](/home/cachebag/rafael/crates/tools/systemd/src/lib.rs:20): systemd wrapper.
- [crates/tools/docker](/home/cachebag/rafael/crates/tools/docker/src/lib.rs:18): Docker wrapper.
- [crates/tools/tailscale](/home/cachebag/rafael/crates/tools/tailscale/src/lib.rs:16): Tailscale wrapper.

What does not exist yet:

- A unified `ToolExecutor` trait.
- A central registry that maps tool names to executable Rust handlers.
- Chat support for model tool calls.
- A loop like: model asks for tool -> backend executes -> result goes back to model -> model continues.
- UI rendering for tool activity/results.

`services/coding` has its own constrained JSON action loop for repo edits, but it is not using `tool-registry` as a generic tool runtime. It manually prompts for actions like `read_file`, `edit_file`, etc.

So the current architecture is:

```text
tool-registry = schema/name registry scaffold
crates/tools/* = reusable low-level capability crates
services/coding = bespoke tool loop for code edits
services/chat = no tool calling yet
```

A good first real “agent tool” would probably be something low-risk and read-only, like:

```text
system_status
```

It could return:
- active systemd services
- loaded llama model
- GPU memory
- disk usage
- maybe Tailscale status

That would let us build the full tool execution path without starting with dangerous write/shell capabilities.

## User

i don mind starting with something a bit more useful. like web search? what do you think?

## Assistant

Web search is a good first real tool. It is useful immediately, read-only, and it exercises the agent loop without letting the model mutate your machine.

I would build it as two tools, not one:

```text
web_search(query, max_results)
  -> returns titles, URLs, snippets, source/domain, maybe published date

fetch_url(url)
  -> fetches one selected page, extracts readable text, caps bytes
```

That split matters. Search gives candidates; fetch gives evidence. It also keeps the model from blindly ingesting 10 full pages every time.

For provider, I’d choose one of these:

```text
Best homelab-ish option: SearXNG
  self-hosted, no per-query API bill, private-ish, but more maintenance

Best simple API option: Brave Search API
  straightforward, good enough, external dependency

Avoid first: scraping Google/DDG directly
  brittle, rate-limited, annoying
```

Architecture-wise, I’d do this before wiring it into chat:

```text
crates/tools/web
  WebSearchClient
  SearchResult
  FetchedPage
  provider adapter

crates/tool-registry
  add executor shape, not just definitions

services/chat
  model asks for tool
  backend executes tool
  result is appended into model context
  model continues answering
```

The tool should be strict:

- max 5 results by default
- hard timeout
- only `http`/`https`
- cap fetched text, maybe 32-64 KB
- strip scripts/styles
- return citations/URLs
- never let the model browse local addresses like `127.0.0.1`, `192.168.*`, Tailscale IPs, router/admin panels, etc.

That last point is important. A web fetch tool can become SSRF if you let the model request arbitrary URLs.

So yes: web search is more useful than a system-status toy, and still safe enough if we build it read-only and constrained. I’d start with `web_search` first, then `fetch_url`, then wire the full tool loop into chat.

## User

perfect! yes i agree. im gonna let you program this but please respeect my architecture...ive got a lot of ideas and maybe i need to be checked...so tell me what you think about this...

Eventually, ill build out memory, which is important for powerful agents. im thinking wouldn't it be cool when a agent uses our web seearch tool, that we cache literally everyting in a pipelien that gets fed directly into our memory crate? i say this because this could be a "training shim" of sorts. its not actually training, but it does feed our models with the information that otherwise would be annoying to re-fetch. this could be built out in such a way that regardless of waht model uses the web search tool, all key results/findigns gt fed through a pipelien going right into the memory crates mouth.

does that make sense? i havent seen any other OSS tool do his unless i am mistaken

## Assistant

Yes, it makes sense. I’d just sharpen the design: don’t feed “literally everything” directly into long-term memory. Capture everything useful, then promote selectively.

The architecture I’d aim for:

```text
web_search / fetch_url tool
  -> raw evidence cache
  -> extraction / cleanup / dedupe
  -> memory ingestion candidates
  -> durable memory store
  -> future agents retrieve from memory before re-searching
```

The important distinction:

- **Evidence cache:** raw-ish fetched pages, snippets, URL, title, timestamp, content hash. This can be broad and disposable.
- **Memory:** distilled, deduped, source-linked facts/findings that are likely useful later. This should be curated by rules or model-assisted summarization.

So yes, this becomes a kind of “training shim,” but more accurately it is **retrieval-augmented institutional memory**. You are not training the model weights. You are building a local knowledge layer that every model can use.

I would put the pipeline behind the tool layer, not inside any one model/service:

```text
services/chat
  -> tool runtime
  -> crates/tools/web
  -> emits ToolObservation / WebEvidence event
  -> crates/memory ingests it
```

That means Gemma, Qwen, GPT-OSS, coding agents, future automations, etc. all benefit from the same accumulated knowledge.

The checks I’d put on the idea:

1. **Do not let search spam poison memory.**
   A random weak search result should not become long-term memory. Treat it as a candidate first.

2. **Always store provenance.**
   Every memory derived from web should know: URL, title, fetched time, query, model/tool run, content hash.

3. **Separate freshness from memory.**
   “Current president” type facts need expiry or refresh metadata. Some memories should become stale automatically.

4. **Dedupe aggressively.**
   Same URL, canonical URL, and content hash should avoid repeated ingestion.

5. **Keep raw cache bounded.**
   Store fetched pages for a while, but long-term memory should be compact summaries/chunks, not endless full-page dumps.

6. **Make memory queryable by all services.**
   Eventually `services/chat` and `services/coding` should both ask `crates/memory` before calling web search.

A first version could be simple:

```text
crates/tools/web:
  web_search()
  fetch_url()

crates/memory:
  WebEvidence
  MemoryCandidate
  ingest_web_evidence()

data/memory/web-cache/
data/memory/facts/
```

Then later you add embeddings, ranking, summaries, expiry, and source trust.

I won’t claim nobody in OSS does this, but your instinct is solid. Many tools do search, many do vector memory, fewer treat every tool observation as a shared cross-model memory ingestion stream with provenance and freshness. That is a strong architecture for a homelab agent system.
